answers.question: How do I build a city?
answers.response:
Short answer: You “upgrade” one of your own settlements on the board by paying 2 grain (wheat) and 3 ore, then replace that settlement piece with a city piece from your supply. You may do this during your turn in the trading/building phase, provided you have an available city piece.

Details and relevant rules:
- Cost and timing: Building a city costs 2 grain + 3 ore and is done on your turn during the trading/building phase (after you roll). You can build any number of things in any order as long as you have the resources and pieces. (Catan base game rulebook, “Building” – Build a City)
- Where: You must upgrade an existing settlement you already own; you cannot place a city on an empty intersection. You cannot build a city for another player.
- Pieces/limits: You have 4 city pieces. You cannot build more than 4 cities. When you upgrade, remove your settlement from the board and return it to your personal supply (so you can use it later). (Component limits section)
- No road requirement: Because you are upgrading an existing settlement, you do not need to build any new roads to make the upgrade.
- Effects: A city is worth 2 victory points (instead of 1 for a settlement) and when an adjacent terrain hex produces, you take 2 resource cards of that terrain’s type instead of 1. (Production and Victory Points sections)

Common questions and clarifications:
- Can I build a settlement and then immediately upgrade it to a city in the same turn? Yes, as long as you can pay both costs and have the pieces. This is an officially accepted ruling (Catan FAQ/official rulings).
- Can I “downgrade” a city or move it? No—once upgraded, you cannot downgrade or relocate it.
- Do ports change? Your port access is unchanged; if the upgraded settlement was on a harbor, the city still has that port.

Sources:
- Catan base game rulebook (5th ed. and later), “Building – Build a City,” component limits, production, and victory point rules.
- Catan official FAQ: Upgrading a newly built settlement to a city in the same turn is allowed.

answers.question: What happens when I run out of roads?
answers.response:
You can’t build any more. In Catan, you’re limited by the physical pieces in your supply. If you’ve placed all 15 of your roads, you may not build additional roads until you somehow have road pieces back—roads are never removed or moved in the base game, so in practice you’re capped at 15.

Relevant rules:
- Official rules (Base Game, “Building”): You may only build if you have both the resources and the corresponding building pieces available in your supply. Once placed, roads stay on the board and cannot be moved.
- Development card interaction (Road Building): If you play Road Building but don’t have two roads left, you may place only as many as you have available (including zero).

Notes:
- This is the official rule. Some groups use house rules (e.g., proxy pieces or moving roads), but those are not official.
- Longest Road is unaffected by “running out”—you can still hold/claim it with your existing network; you just can’t extend it further without available road pieces.

answers.question: If I have multiple VP development cards, can I play them all in the same turn?
answers.response:
Yes. You may reveal any number of Victory Point (VP) development cards on your turn, and they do not count against the “one development card per turn” limit.

Relevant rules:
- Base rules (Development Cards): You may play only one development card per turn and not on the same turn you bought it—except Victory Point cards. VP cards may be revealed at any time during your turn, including the turn you buy them, and they don’t count as your one card play.
- Winning (Victory Conditions): You can only win on your own turn, so you reveal VP cards on your turn to reach 10+ points.

Official vs. house rules:
- Official rulebook/FAQ: Multiple VP cards can be revealed in the same turn; they’re exempt from the one-card limit.
- Common house rule: Some groups restrict all development cards (including VP) to one per turn. This is not official.

answers.question: How can the Robber get activated?
answers.response:
Short answer:
- Roll a 7 on the production dice.
- Play a Knight (formerly called Soldier) development card.

Relevant rules (Base Catan, 5th Edition):
- Rolling a 7 (Rulebook: “If you roll a 7,” typically p. 7): All players with more than 7 resource cards discard half (rounded down). The roller must move the robber to a different terrain hex and may steal 1 random resource from a player with a settlement/city adjacent to that hex.
- Playing a Knight development card (Rulebook: “Development Cards—Knight,” typically p. 9): On your turn, you may play a Knight to move the robber to another terrain hex and steal 1 random resource from an adjacent opponent. No one discards for hand size in this case. Playing Knights also counts toward Largest Army.

Notes/edge cases:
- You must move the robber to a new hex; you cannot leave it where it is (official rule). The desert is a legal destination. If it’s already on the desert, you still must move it elsewhere.
- You may move the robber multiple times in the same turn if you both rolled a 7 and later play a Knight.
- Earlier printings name the card “Soldier”; it functions identically.

Expansions (for clarity; not part of base Catan):
- Cities & Knights removes Knight development cards; instead, certain Progress cards (e.g., Bishop) move the robber, and rolling a 7 still moves the robber. The barbarian attack is a separate mechanic. If you need expansion-specific details, say which one you’re using.

answers.question: When does the game end?
answers.response:
Short answer (base game): The game ends immediately when, on your turn, you reach 10 Victory Points. You may only reveal Victory Point development cards on your own turn; once your total is 10+, you win at once.

Relevant rules
- Base Catan rulebook, “End of the Game” section: “If you have 10 or more victory points during your turn, you win!” The win is immediate when you reach the threshold—e.g., by building a settlement/city, claiming Longest Road/Largest Army, or revealing VP development cards. VP cards may be revealed only on your turn and do not count against the 1-dev-card-per-turn limit.
- You cannot win on another player’s turn. You can only build/claim awards/reveal VP cards on your own turn, so even if standings change between turns, victory is only checked on your turn.

Expansions and variants (official)
- 5–6 Player Extension: Same rule; threshold is still 10 VP in the base game.
- Seafarers: Most scenarios set a different VP target (often 12). The “End of the Game” for each scenario specifies the required VP; the win is still immediate on your turn when you reach that total.
- Cities & Knights: The target is 13 Victory Points. The game ends immediately when a player reaches 13 on their turn (e.g., through metropolises, progress, or Defender of Catan points, which are awarded during the active player’s turn).

Ambiguities / common house rules
- Some groups play “finish the round” (everyone gets an equal number of turns) or “win at end of your turn” to reduce first-player advantage. These are house rules and not part of the official rules.
- A rare dispute is whether Longest Road/Largest Army can cause someone to “lose” or “gain” and end the game between turns. Officially, those awards change hands when the condition is achieved—i.e., during the active player’s turn; you still can’t win outside your turn.

If you want the exact wording, check “End of the Game” in the Catan base rulebook (5th Edition and later). If you’re playing a scenario/expansion, use the VP target and end condition printed in that scenario’s rules.

answers.question: Can I "give away" resources with the following trade scheme? Player A trades 2 sheep for 1 wheat with player B. Then, Player A trades 1 wheat for 1 sheep with player B.
answers.response:
Short answer: Yes, this is legal in base Catan rules if both trades occur on Player A’s turn and each trade exchanges at least one card from each side.

Relevant rules:
- Domestic trade rules (Catan 5th ed. Rulebook, “Trade” section): On your turn you may make any number of trades with other players. All trades must be with the active player and must be immediately fulfilled.
- “No gifts” rule (same section): You may not give away resource cards; each trade must involve both players handing over at least one resource card.

Your sequence (2 sheep for 1 wheat, then 1 wheat for 1 sheep) satisfies both conditions: each exchange involves cards from both sides, and they can both happen on A’s turn. While the net effect is that B gains a sheep, the rules do not require trades to be “fair” or prevent back‑to‑back trades.

Ambiguity/contested interpretations:
- Some players view this as a “gift in two steps” and against the spirit of the no‑gift rule. However, the most commonly accepted interpretation is that it’s permitted as long as each individual trade is a valid, immediate exchange involving the active player.

Official vs. house rules/tournament rules:
- Official rules: Allow it, per above.
- Organized play/tournaments: Many Catan tournament/organized play policies prohibit collusion/kingmaking and “resource dumping” intended solely to help another player win. A judge could disallow blatantly one‑sided chains of trades if they constitute collusion.
- Common house rules: Some groups ban obviously lopsided trades or back‑to‑back swaps intended as gifts; if your group prefers that, agree on it before play.

If you want to ensure compliance everywhere: keep both trades on A’s turn, make them publicly declared, and avoid patterns that look like collusion in competitive settings.

answers.question: How many VPs would this be: 4 settlements, 1 city and longest road?
answers.response:
8 Victory Points.

- Settlements: 4 × 1 VP = 4 VP (Catan base rulebook, Victory Points; each settlement is worth 1 VP).
- City: 1 × 2 VP = 2 VP (a city is worth 2 VP; it replaces a settlement) .
- Longest Road: 2 VP (Special Card “Longest Road,” worth 2 VP while you hold it).

Total: 4 + 2 + 2 = 8 VP.

Official rules reference: Base Game Rulebook — “End of the Game/Victory Points” and “Longest Road” sections (also shown on the Building Cost cards).

answers.question: How are ties broken if multiple people reach 10 VPs at the same time?
answers.response:
Short answer: There are no ties in Catan. The first player to reach 10 victory points on their own turn wins immediately and the game ends.

Relevant rules:
- End of the Game: “As soon as you reach 10 victory points on your turn, you win.” (Base Game rulebook, End of the Game section)
- Victory Point development cards: These remain hidden and can only be revealed on your own turn; they do not count until you reveal them then. (Development Cards section)
- Largest Army / Longest Road: These awards are (re)claimed during a player’s turn when the conditions are met. They can’t cause someone else to win “out of turn.”

Implications:
- If multiple players could have 10+ VPs at the same moment (e.g., via hidden VP cards or shifting Longest Road), only the current player can reveal/claim and win. Others cannot win outside their own turn.
- If the current player’s action both gives them 10 and knocks another player down (e.g., by taking Longest Road), the current player wins immediately.

Ambiguity/house rules:
- Official rules do not provide a tie-break or “finish the round.” Some groups house-rule finishing the round and then breaking ties (e.g., most resources, most cities, longest road length), but that is not official.

Official vs. common practice:
- Official rule: Immediate win at 10 VPs on your turn (rulebook: End of the Game; Development Cards).
- Common house rule: Finish the round or use tie-breakers—optional, not official.

answers.question: When discarding cards for the Robber, do development cards count toward hand size?
answers.response:
Short answer: No. Only resource cards count toward the “7” discard.

Relevant rule:
- Base game (5th ed.) rulebook, “Rolling a ‘7’”: Each player who has more than 7 resource cards must discard half (rounded down) of their resource cards. Development cards are not resource cards and are not counted or discarded.

Clarifications:
- Official FAQs from Catan Studio reiterate that development cards do not count toward the hand size for discarding on a 7.
- Expansions:
  - Seafarers: Same as base—only resource cards count.
  - Cities & Knights: You must count and potentially discard both resource and commodity cards; progress (development-style) cards still do not count.

This is an official rule, not a house rule.

answers.question: Can new settlements be built on existing roads?
answers.response:
Short answer: No. Settlements are placed on intersections (the corners where three hexes meet), not on road segments (edges).

Relevant rules:
- Official rules (Base Game Rulebook, “Building a Settlement”): A settlement must be placed on an open intersection that:
  - Is connected to your own road network (i.e., one of your roads must lead to that intersection), except during initial placement.
  - Obeys the Distance Rule: it must be at least 2 edges away from any existing settlement or city (no adjacent intersections).
- Roads occupy edges; settlements/cities occupy intersections. You cannot “upgrade” or place a settlement on top of a road segment.

Common confusion and clarifications:
- You may place a settlement at the end of an existing road (on the intersection it reaches), but not on the road piece itself.
- Placing a settlement at an intersection can block other players from extending their roads through that intersection; roads cannot pass through an occupied intersection.

Official errata/FAQ: No errata changes this rule.

Common house rules (not official): Some groups allow “converting” a road into a settlement at a reduced cost or replacing the road piece at an intersection; this is not supported by the official rules.

answers.question: What happens when the birds turmoil for the 4th time?
answers.response:
Short answer: Nothing special. You resolve Turmoil as normal, then reset your Leaders.

Relevant rules:
- Turmoil (Eyrie Dynasties): If you cannot complete your Decree, you Turmoil. You lose 1 victory point per bird‑suited card in your Decree, excluding the two Loyal Viziers; then discard all cards from the Decree (the Viziers remain and will be repositioned by the new Leader), and choose a new Leader. [Eyrie player board “Turmoil” box; Law of Root, Eyrie, Decree/Turmoil section]
- Choosing a new Leader: Flip your current Leader face down (in Disgrace), then pick a face‑up Leader. If no Leaders are face up, flip all Leaders face up and choose any Leader. [Leader rules on Eyrie board; Law of Root]

So when you Turmoil for the 4th time (i.e., you’ve just disgraced the last remaining face‑up Leader), there are no face‑up Leaders; you immediately flip all four back face up and choose any of them as your new Leader. There is no extra penalty beyond the normal Turmoil VP loss and Decree reset.

Notes:
- Official rules/FAQ: As above; no escalating penalty for repeated Turmoil.
- Common house rules: Some groups house‑rule increasing VP loss or a lockout on immediately re‑choosing the same Leader. These are not in the official rules.

answers.question: What are the types of Corvid plots?
answers.response:
Bomb, Extortion, Snare, and Raid.

These are the four distinct plot tokens the Corvid Conspiracy can place facedown; each has a different effect when revealed (see Underworld Expansion rulebook, Corvid Conspiracy – “Plots” section).

answers.question: What is the difference between buildings, tokens, and units?
answers.response:
Short answer
- Buildings: Immobile pieces that go in building slots of a clearing. They usually provide abilities/crafting and often score when placed. They are not units.
- Tokens: Immobile markers placed in clearings or on paths as specified. They don’t use building slots (unless a faction explicitly says so) and generally don’t provide crafting. They are not units.
- Units: Mobile pieces that move and fight. In current rules, “units” means warriors and pawns. They take hits first in battle and count (with buildings) for ruling a clearing.

Relevant rules and distinctions (Root, Law of Root)
- Pieces: The game’s pieces are warriors, buildings, tokens (and in later printings, pawns). Law of Root, Terms/Components.
- Units: Defined as warriors and pawns (Marauder/updated Law of Root). Units can move and be involved in battle.
- Buildings:
  - Placed only in open building slots of a clearing unless a faction rule says otherwise (e.g., some factions place specific tokens in slots—exception, not the norm). Law of Root, Building Slots.
  - Provide ongoing functions and often crafting (e.g., Marquise Workshops, Eyrie Roosts, WA Bases, Lizard Gardens). Faction boards specify which buildings craft.
  - Score VP when placed for most factions.
  - Do not move. In battle, buildings can only be removed after all enemy warriors in that clearing are removed. Law of Root, Battle: Assigning Hits.
  - Contribute to rule. Law of Root, Rule: “You rule a clearing if you have the most warriors and buildings there” (tokens do not count unless a faction says otherwise).
- Tokens:
  - Placed per faction rules; most do not require slots (e.g., Sympathy, Plots, Mob, Keep, Ruins, Wood, Relics). Some exceptions explicitly use slots (e.g., Riverfolk Trade Posts are tokens placed in open building slots).
  - Usually don’t provide crafting unless a faction explicitly ties crafting to them (most crafting comes from buildings shown on faction boards).
  - Do not move. In battle, tokens can be removed after all enemy warriors are gone (same removal order as buildings). Law of Root, Battle: Assigning Hits.
  - Do not contribute to rule unless a faction says they do (the default is they don’t). Law of Root, Rule.
- Units (warriors and pawns):
  - Move via Move actions and can initiate/defend in battles. Law of Root, Move and Battle.
  - Take hits first in battle before any buildings/tokens are removed. Law of Root, Battle: Assigning Hits.
  - Contribute to rule (warriors always do; pawns are units and count if the rules treat them as pieces in the clearing—current rules treat pawns as units that can affect rule; check specific pawn rules if using hirelings). Law of Root, Rule.

Notes and examples
- Examples of buildings: Marquise Sawmill/Workshop/Recruiter; Eyrie Roosts; Woodland Alliance Bases; Lizard Gardens; Lord of the Hundreds Stronghold.
- Examples of tokens: Woodland Alliance Sympathy; Corvid Plot tokens; Marquise Keep; Riverfolk Trade Posts (token that uses a building slot); Hundreds Mobs; Ruins; Relics; Wood.
- Units: All faction warriors; pawns such as the Vagabond and hireling pawns (Marauder).

Sources
- Law of Root (latest printing with Marauder/Exiles & Partisans terminology): Pieces/Units definition; Rule of a Clearing; Building Slots; Battle—Assigning Hits.
- Faction boards specify which pieces are buildings or tokens and whether they craft or score.

If you want, tell me which factions you’re playing and I can list exactly which of their pieces are buildings, tokens, and units.

answers.question: If I strike another faction piece as the Vagabond, does that make my relationship hostile with that faction?
answers.response:
Short answer: Only if you remove a warrior.

- By the Law of Root (Vagabond – Relationships), you become Hostile to a faction “if you remove one of their warriors, by any means (including outside battle).” This includes using a Crossbow/Strike to remove a warrior in your clearing.
- Removing a building or token does not make you Hostile.

References
- Law of Root (2nd ed), Vagabond: Relationships/Hostile: Becoming Hostile if you remove a faction’s warrior, even outside battle.
- Official FAQ/Errata: Confirms that using the Crossbow (Strike) to remove a warrior makes that faction Hostile; removing non‑warrior pieces does not.

Notes
- Once Hostile, you cannot improve relationship with that faction for the rest of the game (aid won’t move their marker).
- You do not need to battle to trigger Hostile; the removal alone is sufficient.

answers.question: When can an ambush card be used?
answers.response:
Short answer:
- Only during a battle, immediately after the attacker declares the target clearing and defender but before dice are rolled.
- The defender may play 1 Ambush card whose suit matches the clearing (Bird Ambush counts as any suit). Then the attacker may play 1 matching Ambush to cancel it. If not canceled, the defender immediately deals 2 hits to the attacker, then discard the Ambush. If the attacker has no warriors left after those hits, the battle ends.

Relevant rules:
- Official rules (Law of Root / Rulebook: Battle sequence, Ambush step): After the attacker declares a battle, the defender may play an Ambush that matches the clearing’s suit (Bird is wild). The attacker may then play a matching Ambush to cancel. If it resolves, Ambush deals 2 immediate hits to the attacker before rolling dice; hits are assigned as normal for that matchup (e.g., to warriors, or to the Vagabond’s items if the Vagabond is the target). Each side can play at most one Ambush per battle.
- You cannot play Ambush at any other time (not during movement, not in Birdsong, and the attacker cannot “ambush offensively”; the attacker’s Ambush can only cancel the defender’s Ambush).
- The defender may Ambush even if they have no warriors in the clearing (as long as they are the chosen defender with a piece there). If the Ambush removes all attacking warriors, the battle ends immediately with no dice rolled.

Notes and clarifications:
- Official FAQ/clarifications (commonly cited): Ambush is allowed even if the defender has zero warriors present; it still deals 2 hits to the attacker. If multiple players have pieces in the clearing, only the chosen defender may Ambush. Each side is limited to one Ambush in that battle.
- Woodland Alliance, Vagabond, and other factions follow the same timing. Against the Vagabond, Ambush hits damage items as normal. Bird Ambush can always be used because it matches any clearing.

If you want exact citations: see the Battle section’s Ambush step in the Root Rulebook / The Law of Root (Ambush timing and cancellation), and the FAQ entry on Ambush with zero defending warriors.

answers.question: How does alliance revolt interact with the Lost City landmark?
answers.response:
Short answer
- Lost City makes its clearing count as all three suits. For Woodland Alliance:
  - Paying the cost: Any two suited supporters (of any suits; they do not have to match each other) can pay for a Revolt in Lost City, and birds are still wild.
  - Placing a base: After removing enemy pieces, you choose which base (fox/rabbit/mouse) to place there, as if the clearing were of that suit. You must have that base token available (you cannot place a base you already have on the map).
  - Crafting afterward: A base (or any crafting piece) in Lost City can be used to craft cards of any suit, since the clearing counts as all suits.

Relevant rules
- Lost City (Landmark, Exiles & Partisans): The clearing with Lost City “is of all three suits.” This applies to suit-matching for costs, placement, movement/battle restrictions, rule, and crafting.
- Woodland Alliance — Revolt (Root Law / faction board): To Revolt, discard two supporters matching the clearing’s suit (or one bird), then remove all enemy pieces in that clearing and place the base of that suit (if available), then place warriors per the Revolt rules.

Implications and edge cases
- Because Lost City is all suits, any suited supporters “match the clearing,” so you may discard any two suited cards to Revolt there.
- You may Revolt in Lost City even if you already have, say, the printed suit’s base somewhere else, by choosing a different base to place there. If you have no base tokens left to place (all three already on the map), you cannot Revolt there (same as normal).
- Crafting uses the suit(s) of the clearing where your crafting pieces are, so a base in Lost City can satisfy crafting requirements of any suit.

Status of ruling
- Official rules: From the Exiles & Partisans Landmark rules for Lost City (“this clearing is of all three suits”) combined with the Woodland Alliance Revolt text. Leder Games’ rules clarifications have confirmed that in Lost City the Alliance may choose which base to place when revolting there and may pay the cost with any suited supporters.
- Common house rules: None widely used that differ here.

answers.question: Which factions can lose Victory Points?
answers.response:
Short answer:
- Eyrie Dynasties can lose VP from Turmoil.
- Any faction can lose VP due to certain crafted card effects (notably Brutal Tactics) or if a VP‑granting Improvement they crafted is removed (e.g., by Saboteurs).

Details and rules references:
- Eyrie Dynasties: During Turmoil, the Eyrie “loses 1 VP per Bird card in the Decree” before resetting (Eyrie player board, Turmoil; Root Law: Eyrie §Turmoil). This is a true loss: move your score marker backward.
- Brutal Tactics (Exiles & Partisans deck): “In battle you initiated, after rolling, you may take +1 hit. If you do, lose 1 VP.” Any faction that crafted this may voluntarily spend 1 VP for the extra hit (card text; Root Law: Crafting/Improvements).
- Saboteurs (Exiles & Partisans deck): “Remove an Improvement.” If that Improvement showed VP in its corner, its crafter immediately loses that many VP (card text; Root Law: Removing Crafted Improvements). Note: This does not affect crafted Items—the VP from Items are never lost, because the card is discarded when the Item is crafted and Saboteurs cannot remove Items.

Common clarifications:
- Losing pieces/buildings/tokens never makes you lose VP; instead, the remover usually gains VP (Core Rules §Combat/Scoring). For example, removing a Trade Post, Sympathy, or building gives the attacker VP, but the defender’s score does not go down.
- Vagabond relationship changes or damaged items do not cause VP loss; VP from quests, Aid thresholds, and crafted items are permanent once gained (Vagabond rules).
- Other factions (Marquise, Woodland Alliance, Riverfolk, Lizard Cult, Duchy, Corvids, Lord of the Hundreds, Keepers in Iron) have no faction rule that deducts VP. They can only lose VP via the card effects noted above.

Summary:
- Faction rule loss: Eyrie (Turmoil).
- Card-based loss available to anyone: Brutal Tactics use; Saboteurs removing a VP‑granting Improvement.

answers.question: Can the Riverfolk Company use the Tunnels card to move between trade posts?
answers.response:
No. Tunnels only links clearings that contain Tunnel tokens from the Underground Duchy, not Riverfolk trade posts.

- Relevant rule: The Tunnels improvement (Exiles & Partisans deck) reads that you may move between clearings with tunnels. “Tunnels” here refers specifically to the Underground Duchy’s tunnel tokens (Underworld Expansion, Underground Duchy rules), which are a distinct token type.
- Riverfolk trade posts are buildings (Riverfolk Company rules) and are not tunnels or tunnel tokens, so they do not create adjacency for Tunnels.
- If the Underground Duchy isn’t in the game (so no tunnel tokens exist), Tunnels’ movement effect does nothing; you still gain its craft VP as normal.

Official ruling: Confirmed in Root Law/FAQ that Tunnels interacts only with Duchy tunnel tokens, not with Riverfolk trade posts. Common house rule: Some groups loosely allow “trade-post-to-trade-post” movement with Tunnels, but this is not supported by the official rules.

answers.question: Can I purchase mercenaries and use them for the dominance victory condition on the same turn?
answers.response:
Short answer: No. You can buy Mercenaries and use them that turn, but they cannot help you win a Dominance victory on that same turn—or at all.

Why:
- Dominance timing: When you craft/flip a Dominance card, your win condition changes, but you only check it at the start of your Birdsong on your future turns, not immediately. Law of Root (Dominance) states you win “at the start of your Birdsong” if you rule the listed clearings. So you cannot achieve the win on the turn you play the Dominance card.
- Mercenaries don’t grant rule: The Riverfolk “Mercenaries” service lets the buyer treat up to two Riverfolk warriors as their own only for moving and initiating battle for the rest of that turn. They do not count for ruling a clearing. This is specified in the Riverfolk rules/Services (Mercenaries) and reiterated in official clarifications.
- Duration mismatch: Even if Mercenaries did count (they don’t), their effect ends when your turn ends, so they wouldn’t be present for the “start of Birdsong” dominance check on your next turn.

Ambiguities and rulings:
- Ambiguity: Some players think Dominance checks immediately when played. Official rule: It does not; it checks only at the start of your Birdsong (Law of Root – Dominance).
- Ambiguity: Some groups let Mercenaries contribute to rule. Official ruling and common consensus: They do not count for rule; they’re only usable for move/battle during your turn (Riverfolk – Mercenaries; Leder Games FAQ/official clarifications).

Official vs. house rules:
- Official rules/FAQ: Dominance checks at the start of your Birdsong; Mercenaries don’t help with rule.
- Common house rule (not official): Allowing Mercenaries to contribute to rule. If used, make sure your table agrees beforehand; it materially strengthens Dominance plays against non-Riverfolk players.

answers.question: As the Scoundrel, can I Scorched Earth the clearing with the Marquise' Keep?
answers.response:
Short answer: Yes. You can use Scorched Earth in the Keep’s clearing. You will remove all removable enemy pieces/tokens there and then place the Flame token; the Keep itself stays.

Relevant rules:
- Scoundrel (Exiles & Partisans Vagabond) – Scorched Earth: Once per game, you remove all enemy pieces/tokens in your clearing and then place the Flame token in that clearing (Exiles & Partisans, Scoundrel card/rules). The effect removes what it can, then you place the Flame.
- Marquise de Cat – Keep: The Keep building cannot be removed except by battle (Law of Root/Marquise rules, Keep ability). Since Scorched Earth is not a battle, the Keep is not removed by it.

What this means in play (official):
- You may target the Keep’s clearing. Remove all Cat warriors/buildings/tokens there that are not protected by the Keep’s “only by battle” clause; the Keep remains.
- You still place the Flame token afterward. While the Flame is present, the clearing is “shut down” per the Scoundrel rules (e.g., no placing pieces there). This prevents the Cats from recruiting there or using Field Hospitals to place warriors at the Keep’s clearing while the Flame persists.

Ambiguities you might see:
- Some players think the Keep prevents any removals in its clearing; officially, only the Keep itself is protected from non-battle removal. Other Cat pieces are removed by Scorched Earth.
- Some ask whether you can place the Flame if the Keep remains. The common and officially accepted reading is yes: you remove what you can and then place the Flame.

If you need exact page cites for your printing, let me know which edition (Law of Root version) you’re using; wording varies slightly between printings, but the interaction above is the official ruling.

answers.question: If a player qualifies for multiple nobles, can they take all of them on their turn?
answers.response:
Short answer: No. You may claim at most one noble per turn.

Relevant rule: In the official Splendor rulebook (Nobles section), it states that at the end of your turn, if you meet the requirements for one or more nobles, you must take exactly one of them (your choice). You cannot take more than one noble in the same turn, even if you qualify for several.

Notes and clarifications:
- Timing: Nobles are checked and taken at the end of your turn, after your action. Their 3 prestige points count immediately for the end‑game trigger (15+ points).
- Mandatory: You cannot refuse a noble if you qualify; you must take one if possible.
- Subsequent turns: If you still meet the requirements on a later turn and the other noble is still available, you may take a different noble then.

Sources:
- Official rulebook: “At the end of your turn, if you have the bonuses indicated, you must take one (and only one) noble tile” (Nobles section; early printings phrase it as “If several nobles can visit you, choose only one.”).
- Official FAQs from Space Cowboys reiterate “one noble per turn.”

This is an official rule; allowing multiple nobles in a single turn is a common house rule but not supported by the rulebook.

answers.question: How do you win a noble?
answers.response:
You “win” (claim) a Noble automatically at the end of your turn if your permanent bonuses from development cards meet that Noble’s requirement.

Key rules (Splendor base game):
- Timing: Check for Nobles at the end of your turn, after resolving your action (taking tokens, buying a card, or reserving and possibly taking a gold). Rulebook: “Nobles” / “End of Turn.”
- Requirement: Only permanent bonuses from cards in your tableau count. Gem tokens (including gold/jokers) do not. Rulebook: Nobles section.
- Automatic and free: If you qualify, you must take one available Noble of your choice; it costs nothing and you cannot refuse. Each Noble is worth 3 Prestige Points.
- One per turn: Even if you qualify for multiple Nobles, you may take only one; choose which one. Rulebook: “Only one Noble can visit a player per turn.”
- Visibility: A number of Noble tiles equal to players + 1 are face up for all to aim at.

Clarifications/edge cases:
- Reserving a card does not grant its bonus; you only gain the bonus (and thus possibly meet a Noble) when the card is actually purchased.
- The check is only at the end of your turn; you don’t claim a Noble in the middle of someone else’s turn or immediately when you hit the requirement mid-turn—resolution happens at end of your turn, then you take one if eligible.

Sources: Splendor rulebook (Space Cowboys), “Nobles” and “End of the Turn” sections; common printings state: “If, at the end of his turn, a player has the bonuses indicated on a Noble tile, he must take it. Only one Noble may visit a player per turn. Tokens do not count.”

answers.question: Can I take 2 gems of the same color if there are only 3 left?
answers.response:
No. You may only take 2 gems of the same color if there are at least 4 of that color in the bank before you take them. With only 3 remaining, that action is not allowed.

Source: Splendor rulebook, “Player’s Turn” actions section (p. 3): “Take 2 gems of the same color (only if there are at least 4 gems of that color left in the bank).”

Notes:
- This applies to the five colored gems (emerald, sapphire, ruby, diamond, onyx), not gold jokers.
- Common house rule: some groups allow taking 2 when exactly 3 remain; this is not official.

answers.question: How many tokens are there in the game?
answers.response:
40 total tokens in the base game.

Breakdown (Splendor rulebook, Components section):
- 7 Emerald (green)
- 7 Sapphire (blue)
- 7 Ruby (red)
- 7 Diamond (white)
- 7 Onyx (black)
- 5 Gold Jokers

Notes:
- This is the official component list in the base game. 
- Gold (joker) tokens are always 5; colored tokens may be reduced during setup for 2–3 players, but the box still contains the full 40.

answers.question: Which cards can I buy on my turn?
answers.response:
Short answer: On your turn, if you choose the “Purchase a development card” action, you may buy exactly one development card that is either:
- Any face-up development card on the table (from any of the three rows), or
- Any development card you have reserved in your hand (including a face-down one you previously reserved from the top of a deck).

Key rules:
- Rulebook, “Taking a turn” → “Purchase a development card”: You may buy one face-up card from the table or one of your reserved cards by paying its cost with gem tokens and/or gold jokers, reduced automatically by your permanent bonuses from previously purchased cards.
- You cannot buy Nobles; they visit automatically if you meet their requirements at the end of your turn (Rulebook, “Nobles”).
- You cannot buy from the top of a deck unless you reserved that specific face-down card earlier.
- You cannot buy a card from another player’s reserved cards.
- You only take one action per turn, so you cannot both take tokens and buy in the same turn; likewise, after you buy and the row refills, you cannot buy the newly revealed card this turn.
- Token limit (10) is checked at the end of your turn; buying can’t be combined with other actions.

Official rules vs. common misunderstandings:
- Official: You must apply permanent bonuses to reduce cost; you then choose which tokens (including gold) to spend for the remaining cost (Rulebook, “Bonuses and costs” under “Purchase a development card”).
- Common misunderstanding (not allowed): Buying a Noble or buying a newly revealed replacement card in the same turn.

answers.question: If I cannot take 3 different colored gem tokens because there are only 2 colors left, each with less than 4 in stock, am I allowed to take just 2?
answers.response:
Yes, but only because a third color isn’t available.

Official rule (Splendor rulebook, Actions – “Take 3 gem tokens of different colors”): You must take three different colors if possible. If fewer than three colors are available in the supply, you may take only as many different colors as are available (e.g., 2 if only two colors remain, 1 if only one color remains). Gold/jokers don’t count as a color for this action.

Notes:
- The “at least 4 in stock” restriction applies only to the separate action “take 2 tokens of the same color,” not to taking different colors.
- You can’t choose to take only 2 if a third color is available; you must take 3 different colors in that case.
- The 10-token limit still applies; discard down to 10 after taking.

answers.question: Can I spend 4 gold tokens to buy a card that costs 4 gems?
answers.response:
Yes. Gold tokens are wild and can replace any required gem when buying a development card, with no limit on how many gold you use for a single purchase.

Relevant rules:
- Rulebook, Buying a Development Card: you pay the cost with your tokens and discounts from bonuses; “gold (joker) tokens can replace any color.”
- Rulebook, Reservation and Gold: you only gain gold by reserving a card, and you may take one gold if available; total tokens in hand (including gold) may not exceed 10.

Notes:
- There is no rule limiting how many gold tokens you can hold beyond the 10-token limit; the practical cap is typically constrained by how often you can reserve (max 3 cards reserved at a time) and gold availability in the bank.
- Official rules allow paying an entire card cost with gold if you have enough.
- Common house rule: some groups limit gold usage (e.g., max 1 gold per purchase). This is not in the official rules.
- Reminder: nobles can only be claimed with permanent bonuses from cards; tokens (including gold) do not count toward nobles.

answers.question: Do reserved cards need to be visible to all other players at the table?
answers.response:
Short answer: No. Reserved development cards are kept hidden in your hand; only the number of cards you have reserved must be visible.

Relevant rules:
- Core rulebook, “Reserving a development card”: “To reserve a card, take it into your hand … You can have at most 3 reserved cards in your hand.” Taking it “into your hand” indicates it is private/hidden information; the examples in the rulebook show reserved cards face down and held in hand.
- Public information: Opponents may see how many cards you have reserved and any gold (joker) tokens you took, but not the identities of the reserved cards. If you reserved a face-up card from the display, everyone did have the chance to see it at the moment you reserved it; after that, you do not have to keep it visible.

Ambiguity/interpretations:
- Some players assume reserved cards should be placed face up in front of you for convenience. That is a common house rule to reduce memory load, but it is not the official rule.

Official vs house rule:
- Official rule (rulebook): Reserved cards are hidden in your hand until purchased; max 3.
- House rule (common): Keep reserved cards face up in front of players to ease tracking; use only if your group prefers open information.

answers.question: What happens when a player cannot do any of the three required actions on their turn?
answers.response:
Short answer: You pass and do nothing; then apply end-of-turn checks (discard down to 10 tokens if needed, and a Noble may still visit if you already meet its requirements).

Relevant rules:
- Core rules (rulebook p. 3) say you must take exactly one action each turn: take tokens, buy a development, or reserve a development (and take a gold if available). Reserving is allowed even if no gold tokens remain; the limit is having at most 3 reserved cards.
- The base rulebook does not explicitly describe “passing,” but the official FAQ/errata from Space Cowboys clarifies that if none of the three actions is legal, the player simply does nothing and ends their turn.
- Nobles (rulebook p. 6) are checked “at the end of your turn,” regardless of whether you took an action; if you already meet a Noble’s requirement, it visits then.
- End-of-turn token limit (rulebook p. 4): if you have more than 10 tokens, you must discard down to 10 at the end of your turn.

Notes and edge cases:
- It’s rare to be unable to act because you can almost always reserve a card (even face-down from a deck). You’re blocked only if: you cannot take tokens legally (insufficient supply or you’re at the 10-token limit), cannot afford any purchase, and already have 3 cards reserved.
- Official rules require you to take an action if you can. A common house rule lets players “pass” voluntarily even when actions are available, but that is not supported by the rulebook.

answers.question: When purchasing cards, can I choose to pay with gem tokens instead of cards or vice versa?
answers.response:
Short answer: No. You don’t choose between “paying with tokens” or “paying with cards.” In Splendor, development cards you’ve bought give permanent discounts (bonuses) that automatically reduce the cost of future cards; they are never spent. After applying all your bonuses, you must pay the remaining cost exactly with gem tokens and/or gold (wild) tokens.

Relevant rules:
- Rulebook, Buying a Development Card: You “pay its cost with your tokens and/or jokers, taking into account any bonuses from cards you have previously acquired.” Bonuses are permanent and cumulative and are not spent; they automatically reduce the cost by 1 of their color per card you own.
- You cannot “overpay”; you pay exactly the remaining cost after discounts.
- You may choose which tokens to spend for the remaining cost, including using a gold token even if you have the matching colored token.

Official clarifications:
- Space Cowboys/Asmodee FAQ: The discount from bonuses is mandatory; you cannot ignore a bonus to spend tokens instead.
- You are free to use gold in place of a colored token even if you have that color, as long as the total paid matches the discounted cost.

Common house rule (not official):
- Some groups allow players to “ignore” a bonus to dump tokens when near the 10-token limit. This is not supported by the official rules.
